response:
I proceeded round-by-round, validating the small “local” bitwise operations before any sums. In particular I checked the choose function ch(e,f,g) = (e and f) xor (~e and g), which is equivalent to bitwise-select “if e bit = 1 then take f’s bit else take g’s bit.”

- Round 31 inputs (L0503): 
  e = 0101 0010 1001 1110 1100 1111 0000 0110
  f = 0100 1000 1101 0001 0110 0111 1110 1100
  g = 1011 0101 0100 1110 1011 1100 1001 0010

- The trace gives (L0505):
  ch = 1110 0101 1101 0000 0111 0111 1001 0100

- Recomputing ch by selection:
  - For the least significant nibble, e_LSB = 0110, f_LSB = 1100, g_LSB = 0010.
    Bitwise-select yields ch_LSB = 0110 (pick f where e has 1s, else g), not 0100.
  - In fact, the full recomputation gives:
    ch = 1110 0101 1101 0000 0111 0111 1001 0110
  This differs from the trace’s value by exactly one bit in the last nibble (…0110 vs …0100).

- Consistency after the tamper point:
  Using the trace’s ch (the incorrect one from L0505), step2 = step1 + ch (L0507) matches exactly when added to step1 (L0506), showing the remainder of round 31 (and thus everything that follows) is computed consistently from the tampered ch.

VERDICT: TAMPERED
ROUND: 31
REASONING: In round 31, ch at L0505 does not equal bitwise-select(ch) from e,f,g at L0503. Recomputing shows the correct ch should be 1110 0101 1101 0000 0111 0111 1001 0110, but L0505 has 1110 0101 1101 0000 0111 0111 1001 0100 (one-bit difference in the last nibble). Step2 (L0507) matches step1 (L0506) plus the incorrect ch, confirming consistent recomputation after the single-bit tamper.
